metadata.title: Why the Wave Crests Move Twice as Fast as the Packet
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = the envelope / packet (the physical particle); CRIMSON = the individual crests (the phase structure that races ahead); never swapped
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no Taylor expansion of omega(k), no Fourier-integral derivation, no dispersion-spreading math. No equations on screen. Wonder register: first principles, genuine curiosity, no drills.

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: Something strange is happening inside this traveling quantum packet. The ripples aren't keeping pace — they race forward, born at the rear, vanishing at the front.

production_viz.label: wave packet traveling right — crests overtaking envelope
production_viz.mechanic: a Gaussian envelope (TEAL filled area) translates right at speed v_g; a sine-wave pattern (CRIMSON) inside the envelope travels at v_ph = v_g/2 so crests visibly emerge from the left side of the envelope and exit through the right; the envelope center is marked with a dot
production_viz.note: cold open — no labels yet, let the motion speak

beats.narration_text: Here's the contrast. A classical pulse — a ripple on a string — moves as one piece. Its wiggles stay locked to the blob. No separation at all.

production_viz.label: classical pulse — wiggles locked to envelope
production_viz.mechanic: a hump (SLATE outline) with internal oscillations travels right; a dot on one crest moves exactly with the envelope; no separation between crest speed and envelope speed

beats.narration_text: Classical physics predicts the speeds match. A quantum wave packet shows them split — crests running at exactly twice the blob's speed. That needs explaining.

card.copy: Why do the crests inside a quantum packet race at twice the speed of the packet itself?

beats.narration_text: The packet is not one wave. It is a crowd — many pure waves with slightly different wavelengths, arranged so they cancel everywhere except inside that one traveling blob.

production_viz.label: superposition of component waves builds the packet
production_viz.mechanic: three sine waves (TEAL, slightly different wavelengths) appear stacked; they then overlay and combine into the TEAL Gaussian blob; annotation: 'many wavelengths added'
production_viz.note: establish the wave packet as a superposition

beats.narration_text: Each of those component waves travels at its own speed. Short-wavelength waves carry higher momentum — and in quantum mechanics, higher momentum means moving faster.

production_viz.label: components at different speeds
production_viz.mechanic: three labeled sine wave segments (TEAL) arranged vertically; the top has the shortest wavelength and a longer velocity arrow; the bottom has the longest wavelength and shorter arrow; labels: 'short wavelength, higher speed' top, 'long wavelength, lower speed' bottom
production_viz.note: velocity spread causes the crest / envelope speed difference

beats.narration_text: Those crests move at what physicists call the phase velocity — the crest's own speed, set by its frequency. For a free quantum particle, it works out to exactly half the particle's true speed.

production_viz.label: phase velocity label on single crest
production_viz.mechanic: a single CRIMSON sine wave travels right; one crest is highlighted as a dot; velocity arrow labeled 'phase velocity' tracks the crest; underneath: 'p / 2m' in MONO font
production_viz.note: PHASE VELOCITY introduced with CRIMSON = the crest / phase structure

beats.narration_text: But the blob — where the particle actually lives — moves at the group velocity. That's the average the momentum components settle into. And it is exactly twice the phase velocity.

production_viz.label: group vs phase velocity arrows
production_viz.mechanic: the TEAL envelope blob sits center; a TEAL arrow labeled 'group velocity = p/m' points right; beside it a shorter CRIMSON arrow labeled 'phase velocity = p/2m' points right; the TEAL arrow is exactly twice as long as the CRIMSON one
production_viz.note: THE key comparison — the ratio is 2:1

beats.narration_text: So the blob races ahead of its own crests. New ones are born at the trailing edge, travel through the whole packet, and vanish at the front. Again and again.

production_viz.label: crests born at back, die at front
production_viz.mechanic: TEAL envelope advancing; CRIMSON crests appear labeled at the rear edge 'born here', travel through to the front edge labeled 'die here'; trail of dots marks individual crest lifetimes inside the packet
production_viz.note: THE MECHANISM beat — the through-traffic of crests

beats.narration_text: Here's what this means for measurement. No detector ever records where a crest is — only where the blob arrives. The phase velocity is real mathematics, but nothing physical tracks it.
beats.scene_description: a detector screen showing single bright dots appearing in a cluster (the packet envelope has passed), with ghostly wave interference lines fading; no visible individual crest marked
beats.image_prompt: B09, a glowing detector screen showing a cluster of bright pinpoint dot-impacts where a quantum particle landed, surrounded by faint concentric ring interference patterns fading outward, dark laboratory background, flat editorial illustration style on warm eggshell ground, no text, no labels, viewed straight-on, soft directional lighting from above-left

beats.narration_text: This split appears throughout nature. Ocean swells do the same thing — crests race ahead of the wave group. But it's the group, not the crests, that carries energy to shore.

production_viz.label: ocean wave group analogy
production_viz.mechanic: a wavy surface (TEAL fills, SLATE outline) shows three crests in a group; the group envelope (TEAL shaded region) advances slowly right while individual crests (CRIMSON markers) advance faster; small arrows mark both speeds
production_viz.note: implication beat — universal in dispersive media

beats.narration_text: For quantum mechanics, the resolution is clean. The particle's location is the blob. Its speed is the group velocity. The crests are pure phase — carrying no particle, no energy.

production_viz.label: blob = particle, crests = phase only
production_viz.mechanic: a large TEAL envelope with CRIMSON crests inside; two bracketing SerifLabels: 'particle here' pointing to the envelope, 'phase only' pointing to a crest; the crest label has a line through it — excluded from physical meaning
production_viz.note: what the two speeds mean physically

beats.narration_text: Let's watch it happen with concrete numbers. Take momentum equal to one unit — illustrative, not real units. Phase velocity: one-half. Group velocity: one. Start the clock. After one second, the blob has moved one full unit. The crests inside? Only half a unit. Every crest that began at the front has traveled clean through the packet and come out the back.

production_viz.label: illustrative example: p=1, v_g=1, v_ph=0.5
production_viz.mechanic: timeline animation: at t=0, TEAL blob with CRIMSON crests numbered 1-3; at t=1, blob has moved 1 unit (TEAL), but crests have only moved 0.5 units (CRIMSON); crest #1 which was at front is now at center; ruler marks distances; MONO labels show the illustrative values
production_viz.note: EXAMPLE act — illustrative values labeled as such

beats.narration_text: The wave crests run at half-speed — that's the phase velocity. The packet itself, carrying the actual particle, moves at full speed — the group velocity. Two speeds, one traveling wave.

card.copy: The crests run at half-speed — the packet runs at full speed.

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
